objective: Add a fail-fast Codex dispatch wrapper so unavailable models fail within a bounded preflight window, write structured evidence, and return an immediate dispatcher-visible failure line.
scope_boundary: Issue #200 planning, execution-scope, review, validation, closeout, and local memory evidence
out_of_scope: Changing GitHub Actions sweep behavior beyond documenting existing direct canary calls
Changing the Python Codex ingestion helper's existing timeout/retry semantics
research_summary: Issue #200 documents Codex CLI dispatches hanging or failing late when the selected model is unavailable. Existing repo code already has a Codex preflight helper and a Python ingestion helper with bounded timeouts, but local dispatcher-style shell invocations still call `codex exec` directly. The implementation should add a reusable wrapper with a five-second model preflight, structured failure logging, and a one-line `CODEX_FAIL` signal, then route future local dispatcher Codex brief calls through that wrapper.

sprints.name: Planning and scope
sprints.goal: Create issue-backed governance artifacts before modifying dispatcher tooling.
acceptance_criteria: Execution scope covers only issue #200 files
Same-family exception documents the no-HITL directive and mitigation

sprints.name: Fail-fast wrapper
sprints.goal: Add `scripts/codex-fire.sh` with bounded preflight, structured logging, and dispatcher-visible failure output.
tasks: Implement argument parsing for model, effort, worktree, and brief
Probe model availability with a timeout before firing the real brief
Return the required `CODEX_FAIL` line on preflight or execution failure
Add deterministic tests with a fake Codex CLI
acceptance_criteria: Preflight model failure exits within five seconds
Failure writes a structured log row
Execution failure writes a structured log row and emits the one-line dispatcher signal
Success path runs the brief through Codex without writing a failure row

sprints.name: Dispatcher routing and closeout
sprints.goal: Route local dispatcher Codex brief calls through the wrapper and prove the behavior end to end.
tasks: Update `scripts/codex-review-template.sh` audit and critique modes to use `codex-fire.sh`
Update the governance Codex-spark memory note
Record focused review and validation evidence
Run local CI and GitHub PR checks before merge
acceptance_criteria: Future local dispatcher Codex brief invocations use `codex-fire.sh`
Existing dedicated preflight and bounded ingestion helpers are documented as non-dispatch exceptions
Memory evidence references `codex-fire.sh` as the required dispatch path
Final AC includes an e2e fake-Codex proof for preflight failure, execution failure, and success

specialist_reviews.focus: Codex wrapper failure semantics, tests, and dispatcher call-site coverage
specialist_reviews.summary: Focused review found that `codex-review-template.sh` initially masked wrapper failures and deleted failed temporary briefs. The template now exits nonzero on wrapper failure, retains failed briefs for inspection, and has regression coverage. Memory update evidence is recorded as local out-of-repo evidence in validation.

alternate_model_review.summary: No separate Claude connector is available in this Codex session and the operator directed no HITL; same-family implementation is accepted for this narrow governance tooling bug with deterministic fake-Codex tests, local CI, GitHub checks, and focused subagent review before merge.

execution_handoff.approved_scope_summary: Proceed with issue #200 Codex dispatch fail-fast wrapper and local dispatcher call-site wiring.
execution_handoff.next_execution_step: Implement the wrapper, tests, dispatcher wiring, memory evidence, validation, and closeout.

material_deviation_rules: Do not edit downstream product repositories.
Do not remove the existing dedicated Codex preflight helper.
Do not make unavailable-model failures silent or unbounded.
If new dispatcher Codex call sites are discovered, either route them through `codex-fire.sh` or document why they are not dispatcher brief invocations.
